payload.summary: This week's review pulse highlights key areas of concern for the app, with 40% of reviews focusing on trading and investment features, 20% on account setup and syncing issues, 20% on customer support and withdrawal issues, and 20% on platform bugs and inaccurate data. Many users have expressed frustration with the lack of certain features, difficulties with account setup and syncing, and problems with customer support. On the other hand, some users have found the app to be easy to use and have appreciated its speed and stability.

action_items: Implement auto TP and SL features in points, not percentages, to address user requests and improve trading experience.
Enhance customer support by providing a live support system, such as a chatbot or phone support, to address user concerns and issues in a timely manner.
Fix bugs and syncing issues to improve the overall user experience and prevent data loss or inaccuracies, ensuring a stable and reliable platform for users.

explanation_bullets: A mutual fund exit load is a fee charged to investors when they redeem their units from a mutual fund scheme.
The exit load is typically expressed as a percentage of the redemption amount.
It is deducted from the redemption proceeds and paid to the mutual fund house.
The purpose of an exit load is to discourage investors from withdrawing their money too quickly, thereby helping the fund manager to maintain a stable portfolio.
The exit load percentage and the time period for which it is applicable vary across different mutual fund schemes.
Investors should check the mutual fund scheme's documents or website to know the applicable exit load structure.
A brokerage fee is a charge levied by a broker or brokerage firm for their services.
It is typically a percentage of the transaction value or a flat fee per transaction.
Brokerage fees can vary depending on the type of investment, such as stocks, bonds, or mutual funds.
These fees are usually deducted from the investor's account at the time of the transaction.
Brokerage fees can be charged for both buying and selling securities.
The fee structure may differ among brokerage firms and may be negotiable in some cases.
